title: “Contemporary” Spiritual Epochs
caption: Table I from The Decline of the West, Vol. I. Each row is one stage of the soul's life; read across to see the figures Spengler holds to be 'contemporary' — at the same point in their Culture's cycle, whatever the calendar date.
source: Decline of the West, Vol. I — Table I, “Contemporary” Spiritual Epochs (at end of volume).

rows.label: Birth of a myth of the grand style, expressing a new God-feeling. World-fear and world-longing.

apollinian.text: Hellenic-Italian “Demeter” religion of the people. Homer. Heracles and Theseus legends.

magian.text: Primitive Christianity (Mandaeans, Marcion, Gnosis, Syncretism: Mithras, Baal). Gospels, Apocalypses. Christian, Mazdaist and pagan legends.

faustian.text: German Catholicism. Edda (Baldr). Bernard of Clairvaux, Joachim of Floris, Francis of Assisi. Popular Epos (Siegfried). Western legends of the Saints.

rows.label: Earliest mystical-metaphysical shaping of the new world-outlook. Zenith of Scholasticism.

indian.text: Preserved in the oldest parts of the Vedas.

apollinian.text: Oldest (oral) Orphic, Etruscan discipline. After-effect: Hesiod, Cosmogonies.

magian.text: Origen (d. 254), Plotinus (d. 269), Mani (d. 276), Iamblichus (d. 330). Avesta, Talmud, Patristic literature.

faustian.text: Thomas Aquinas (d. 1274), Duns Scotus (d. 1308), Dante (d. 1321), Eckhardt (d. 1329). Mysticism. Scholasticism.

rows.label: Reformation: internal popular opposition to the great springtime forms.

indian.text: Brahmanas. Oldest parts of the Upanishads (10th and 9th Centuries).

apollinian.text: Orphic movement. Dionysiac religion. “Numa” religion (7th Century).

faustian.text: Nicolaus Cusanus (d. 1464). John Hus (d. 1415). Savonarola, Karlstadt, Luther, Calvin (d. 1564).
faustian.note: The source OCR misprints Hus's death as 1308; he was burned at Constance in 1415.

rows.label: Beginning of a purely philosophical form of the world-feeling. Opposition of idealistic and realistic systems.

magian.text: Byzantine, Jewish, Syrian, Coptic and Persian literature of the 6th and 7th Centuries.

faustian.text: Galileo, Bacon, Descartes, Bruno, Boehme, Leibniz (16th and 17th Centuries).

rows.label: Formation of a new mathematic. Conception of number as copy and content of world-form.

apollinian.text: Number as magnitude (proportion). Geometry, Arithmetic. Pythagoreans (from 540).

magian.text: The indefinite number (Algebra). (Development not yet investigated.)

faustian.text: Number as Function (analysis). Descartes, Pascal, Fermat (c. 1630). Newton and Leibniz (c. 1670).
English Puritans (from 1620). French Jansenists (from 1640), Port Royal.

rows.label: “Enlightenment.” Belief in the almightiness of reason. Cult of “Nature.” “Rational” religion.

apollinian.text: Sophists of the 5th Century. Socrates (d. 399). Democritus (d. c. 360).

rows.label: Zenith of mathematical thought. Elucidation of the form-world of numbers.
Materialistic world-outlook: cult of science, utility and prosperity.

magian.text: Communistic, atheistic, Epicurean sects of Abbassid times. “Brethren of Sincerity.”

faustian.text: Bentham, Comte, Darwin. Spencer, Stirner, Marx, Feuerbach.

rows.label: Ethical-social ideals of life. Epoch of “unmathematical philosophy.” Skepsis.

faustian.text: Schopenhauer, Nietzsche. Socialism, Anarchism. Hebbel, Wagner, Ibsen.

rows.label: Inner completion of the mathematical form-world. The concluding thought.
